back: Reports that summarize a business’s financial activity and position. The main ones are the income statement, balance sheet, statement of owners equity, and statement of cash flows.
A report that shows whether a business made money over a period of time. Net Income = Revenue − Expenses.
A report that shows what a business owns, owes, and has left for owners at a specific date. Assets = Liabilities + Owners Equity.
A report that explains how the owners’ claim in the business changed during a period. It usually includes investments, net income or loss, and withdrawals or dividends.
A report that shows how cash came in and went out during a period. It separates cash flows into operating, investing, and financing activities.
Resources a business owns or controls that are expected to provide future benefit. Examples include cash, inventory, equipment, and buildings.
Amounts a business owes to others. Examples include loans, accounts payable, wages payable, and taxes payable.
The owners’ claim on the business after debts are subtracted. Owners Equity = Assets − Liabilities.
The basic rule that keeps accounting records balanced. Assets = Liabilities + Owners Equity.
Money earned from selling goods or providing services. Revenue increases net income and usually increases equity.
Costs used to earn revenue during a period. Expenses decrease net income and usually decrease equity.
The profit a business earns when revenues are greater than expenses. Net Income = Revenue − Expenses.
The result when expenses are greater than revenues. Net Loss = Expenses − Revenue.
A business owned by one person. The owner and business are legally closely connected, so the owner is personally responsible for business debts.
A business owned by two or more people. The partners usually share profits, losses, and responsibility for the business.
A business legally separate from its owners. Owners usually have limited liability and own the company through shares of stock.
Protection that keeps owners from usually being personally responsible for business debts. In practice, owners can lose what they invested but not usually their personal assets.
A person or organization that owns shares of a corporation. Stockholders are owners of the company.
Another name for a stockholder. A shareholder owns shares of a corporation.
The main accounting rules used by companies in the United States. GAAP helps companies prepare financial statements in a consistent way.
Accounting rules used in many countries outside the United States. IFRS helps companies around the world prepare financial statements in a comparable way.
A method that records revenues when earned and expenses when incurred, not necessarily when cash changes hands. This gives a better picture of business performance during a period.
A method that records revenue when cash is received and expenses when cash is paid. It is simpler but often gives a less accurate picture of performance.
A 12-month accounting period used by a business. It does not have to match the calendar year.
The specific span of time covered by a financial report. Examples include a month, quarter, or year.
The assumption that a business will continue operating into the future. This matters because many assets are recorded as useful over time instead of being valued as if the business is shutting down.
The rule that many assets are recorded at what the business originally paid for them. This makes records easier to verify.
The assumption that accounting records only include items that can be measured in money. In the United States, financial statements are usually reported in dollars.
The rule that the business’s finances are kept separate from the owner’s personal finances. This helps users see what belongs to the business itself.
The rule that companies should provide important information users need to understand the financial statements. This often appears in notes attached to the statements.
A list of all accounts a business uses to record transactions. It works like a menu of categories for accounting records.
A record used to track increases and decreases in one specific item. Examples include Cash, Accounts Payable, Sales Revenue, and Rent Expense.
An entry on the left side of an account. Debits increase assets and expenses, but decrease liabilities, equity, and revenue.
An entry on the right side of an account. Credits increase liabilities, equity, and revenue, but decrease assets and expenses.
The side of an account where increases are usually recorded. Assets and expenses normally have debit balances, while liabilities, equity, and revenue normally have credit balances.
A recording system where every transaction affects at least two accounts. Total debits must always equal total credits.
The first formal record of a transaction in accounting. It lists the accounts affected, the amounts, and whether each account is debited or credited.
The place where journal entries are first recorded in date order. It shows the story of transactions before they are moved to account records.
The full collection of all accounts used by a business. It shows the running balance for each account.
A simple drawing of an account shaped like a T. Debits go on the left and credits go on the right.
The process of moving amounts from journal entries into the ledger accounts. Posting updates each account’s balance.
A list of accounts and their balances used to check whether total debits equal total credits. It helps catch recording errors before statements are prepared.
A trial balance prepared before adjusting entries are made. It shows account balances after regular transactions have been recorded and posted.
The process of recording a transaction as a journal entry. It identifies which accounts are debited and credited.
Evidence that supports a transaction. Examples include receipts, invoices, checks, bank statements, and sales slips.
A journal entry with more than two accounts. Even with several accounts, total debits still must equal total credits.
Adding a column of numbers to get a total. In accounting, footing is often used to total debits or credits.
Marking records so a journal entry can be traced to the related ledger account. It helps prove that transactions were posted correctly.
An account that reduces the balance of a related account. For example, Accumulated Depreciation reduces the recorded value of equipment.
An account whose balance carries forward from one period to the next. Assets, liabilities, and equity accounts are permanent accounts.
An account that is reset to zero at the end of the period. Revenue, expense, and dividend accounts are temporary accounts.
The repeated process accountants use to record transactions and prepare financial statements. It starts with transactions and ends with closing the books for the period.
The process of deciding how a business event affects accounts. It identifies what increased, what decreased, and which accounts need debits or credits.
Entries made at the end of a period to update accounts before financial statements are prepared. They help record revenues and expenses in the correct period.
A trial balance prepared after adjusting entries are posted. It provides the updated account balances used to prepare financial statements.
Entries made at the end of the period to reset temporary accounts to zero. They move the period’s profit or loss into retained earnings or owner’s equity.
A trial balance prepared after closing entries are posted. It contains only permanent accounts because temporary accounts have been reset.
A temporary account used during the closing process. It collects revenues and expenses before the net income or loss is moved to equity.
Payments of company profits to stockholders. Dividends reduce retained earnings but are not expenses.
The amount of a corporation’s past profits kept in the business instead of paid out as dividends. Ending Retained Earnings = Beginning Retained Earnings + Net Income − Dividends.
Optional entries made at the beginning of a new period to undo certain adjusting entries. They make later routine entries easier to record.
A working paper used to organize trial balances, adjustments, and financial statement amounts. It helps accountants prepare statements but is not itself a formal financial statement.
A company that buys and sells goods to customers. Its income statement includes sales revenue, cost of goods sold, and gross profit.
A company that earns revenue by providing services instead of selling goods. Examples include consulting firms, repair shops, and tutoring businesses.
The cost of inventory that was sold to customers during the period. Gross Profit = Sales Revenue − Cost of Goods Sold.
The profit left after subtracting the cost of goods sold from sales revenue. Gross Profit = Sales Revenue − Cost of Goods Sold.
The regular costs of running a business, not including the cost of the goods sold. Examples include rent, salaries, utilities, and advertising.
An income statement that separates major sections such as gross profit, operating income, and net income. It gives more detail about how profit was earned.
An income statement that groups all revenues together and all expenses together. Net Income = Total Revenues − Total Expenses.
A cost paid in advance before the benefit is used. It starts as an asset and becomes an expense over time.
Cash received before goods or services are provided. It starts as a liability and becomes revenue when the company does the work.
An expense that has been incurred but not yet paid or recorded. It is recorded with an expense and a liability.
Revenue that has been earned but not yet received in cash or recorded. It is recorded with revenue and an asset.
The process of spreading the cost of a long-term asset over the years it is used. It recognizes that assets like equipment wear out or lose usefulness over time.
The total depreciation recorded on a long-term asset so far. It reduces the asset’s reported value on the balance sheet.
The asset’s cost minus total depreciation recorded so far. Book Value = Cost − Accumulated Depreciation.
The estimated amount a company expects to receive for an asset at the end of its useful life. It is used when calculating depreciation.
The estimated length of time a company expects to use a long-term asset. Useful life helps determine how much depreciation is recorded each period.
A method that records the same amount of depreciation each year. Annual Depreciation = (Cost − Salvage Value) ÷ Useful Life.
The idea that expenses should be recorded in the same period as the revenues they helped earn. This helps show the real profit for a period.
The idea that revenue should be recorded when it is earned, not necessarily when cash is received. In practice, this means recording revenue when the company has provided the goods or services.
A situation where cash is paid or received before the related expense or revenue is recorded. Examples include prepaid expenses and unearned revenue.
A situation where revenue or expense is recorded before cash is received or paid. Examples include accrued revenue and accrued expenses.
Money owed to employees for work they have already done. It is a liability until the company pays the employees.
Interest cost a company owes but has not yet paid. It is recorded as a liability.
Interest a company has earned but has not yet collected. It is recorded as an asset.
The cost of supplies used up during the accounting period. It is recorded so the remaining supplies asset is not overstated.
The cost of insurance coverage used during the period. It is often recorded by reducing prepaid insurance.
The cost of using rented space or property during a period. It is recorded when the rent applies to that period.
The portion of a long-term asset’s cost assigned to the current period. It reduces net income but does not directly use cash in that period.
The estimated cost of customers not paying what they owe. It helps match credit sales with the expected loss from unpaid accounts.
A balance sheet that groups assets and liabilities into current and long-term sections. This makes it easier to judge liquidity and solvency.
Assets expected to be used, sold, or turned into cash within one year or one operating cycle. Examples include cash, accounts receivable, inventory, and supplies.
Assets expected to benefit the business for more than one year. Examples include land, buildings, equipment, and long-term investments.
Debts expected to be paid within one year or one operating cycle. Examples include accounts payable, wages payable, and short-term notes payable.
Debts that are not due within the next year. Examples include long-term loans, bonds payable, and long-term lease obligations.
The amount by which current assets exceed current liabilities. Working Capital = Current Assets − Current Liabilities.
A measure of whether a company has enough short-term assets to cover short-term debts. Current Ratio = Current Assets ÷ Current Liabilities.
A company’s ability to pay short-term debts as they come due. Cash and other current assets are important for liquidity.
A company’s ability to survive financially over the long run. Solvency focuses on whether the company can handle its long-term debts.
The owners’ claim on a corporation after liabilities are subtracted from assets. Stockholders Equity = Assets − Liabilities.
The basic ownership shares issued by a corporation. Common stockholders usually have voting rights and may receive dividends.
The amount stockholders paid for stock above its stated or par value. It is part of stockholders’ equity.
The corporation’s accumulated profits kept in the business instead of paid out as dividends. Ending Retained Earnings = Beginning Retained Earnings + Net Income − Dividends.
A broader measure of changes in equity from non-owner sources. It includes net income plus certain gains or losses that do not appear directly on the income statement.
A measure of profit earned for each share of common stock. Earnings Per Share = Net Income Available to Common Stockholders ÷ Average Common Shares Outstanding.
The amount of dividends paid for each share of stock. Dividends Per Share = Total Dividends ÷ Shares Outstanding.
Extra explanations that provide important details behind the financial statement numbers. They can explain accounting choices, risks, debt terms, and other useful information.
Financial statements that show more than one period side by side. They help users see how the company changed over time.
A method that shows each financial statement item as a percentage of a base amount. On an income statement, each item is often shown as a percentage of sales.
A method that compares financial statement amounts across periods. Percentage Change = (New Amount − Old Amount) ÷ Old Amount.
Financial statements that show items as percentages instead of only dollar amounts. They make it easier to compare companies of different sizes.
Policies and procedures designed to protect assets and improve reliable reporting. In practice, they help prevent mistakes, theft, and fraud.
Dividing important tasks among different people. This makes it harder for one person to both commit and hide an error or fraud.
Approval given before a transaction or action takes place. For example, a manager may need to approve large purchases.
Ways to physically protect assets and records. Examples include locks, safes, passwords, cameras, and restricted access.
Checking work by someone who did not originally do it. This helps catch errors and discourage dishonest behavior.
The process of comparing the company’s cash records with the bank statement. It explains differences and updates the cash account.
Checks written by the company that have not yet cleared the bank. They are subtracted from the bank balance during a bank reconciliation.
Deposits recorded by the company that have not yet appeared on the bank statement. They are added to the bank balance during a bank reconciliation.
Fees charged by the bank for account services. They reduce the company’s cash balance and must be recorded in the company’s books.
A customer’s check that the bank refuses because the customer does not have enough money. NSF stands for not sufficient funds.
A small amount of cash kept on hand for minor purchases. It is used for things like postage, small supplies, or quick reimbursements.
The specific amount of money set aside for small cash payments. The fund is replenished when receipts are reviewed and cash is added back.
The person responsible for managing and protecting a petty cash fund or other asset. The custodian keeps records and provides receipts for payments.
Short-term, highly liquid investments that can quickly be turned into cash. They are usually very safe and mature in three months or less.
Cash that is set aside for a specific purpose and not freely available for general use. Companies often disclose restrictions so users understand the cash balance.
A U.S. law passed to improve corporate reporting and reduce financial fraud. In practice, it requires stronger internal controls and more responsibility from top executives.
A model explaining why fraud is more likely when pressure, opportunity, and rationalization are present. Internal controls mainly try to reduce opportunity.
Stealing money or property that a person was trusted to manage. For example, an employee taking company cash for personal use is embezzlement.
Buying insurance protection against losses caused by employee theft or dishonesty. It also often requires background checks before employees handle cash.
Amounts customers owe the company because they bought goods or services on credit. Managers watch this because it affects future cash collections.
Money owed by customers from the company’s normal sales of goods or services. It is usually collected within a short period.
The rules for when a customer must pay after buying on credit. They may include due dates and discounts for early payment.
A credit term meaning the full amount is due within 30 days. It tells the customer how long they have to pay.
A credit term meaning the full amount is due within 60 days. Longer credit periods can help sales but delay cash collection.
The amount of time a customer has to pay after a credit sale. Shorter periods improve cash flow, while longer periods may attract customers.
A method that estimates uncollectible accounts before the exact customers who will not pay are known. It matches bad debt expense with the sales that caused it.
A method that records bad debt only when a specific account is known to be uncollectible. It is simple but usually does not match expenses with the related sales well.
A contra-asset account that estimates how much of accounts receivable will not be collected. It reduces receivables to a more realistic amount.
The estimated cost of customers not paying what they owe. It appears on the income statement as an expense.
The amount a company expects to actually collect from receivables. Net Realizable Value = Accounts Receivable − Allowance for Doubtful Accounts.
A method that groups unpaid customer balances by how long they have been outstanding. Older balances are usually more likely to be uncollectible.
A method that estimates bad debt expense as a percentage of credit sales. Bad Debt Expense = Credit Sales × Estimated Uncollectible Percentage.
The removal of a specific customer account that the company no longer expects to collect. Under the allowance method, it reduces both accounts receivable and the allowance.
Collection of money from a customer account that had previously been written off. The company first reverses the write-off, then records the cash collection.
A written promise that another party will pay the company a specific amount, usually with interest. It is more formal than a regular account receivable.
A written agreement to pay a specific amount by a specific date. It usually names the borrower, lender, interest rate, and maturity date.
The person or company that signs a note and promises to pay. The maker is the borrower.
The person or company that receives payment on a note. The payee is the lender.
The date when a note must be paid. Managers use it to plan expected cash collections.
The original amount borrowed or loaned on a note. Interest is calculated using the principal.
The cost of borrowing money or the income earned from lending money. Interest = Principal × Interest Rate × Time.
The total amount due when a note reaches its maturity date. Maturity Value = Principal + Interest.
A note that is not paid by the maker on the maturity date. The company usually moves it back to accounts receivable if collection is still possible.
Selling receivables to another company to get cash sooner. It improves cash flow but usually costs a fee.
Goods a company owns and plans to sell or use to make products for sale. It is an asset until it is sold or used.
Goods a retailer or wholesaler buys and plans to resell. It becomes cost of goods sold when sold to customers.
Basic materials a manufacturer will use to make products. Examples include wood, fabric, metal, or parts.
Products that have been started but are not finished yet. It includes materials, labor, and overhead already used in production.
Completed products that are ready to be sold. They stay in inventory until sold to customers.
An inventory system that updates inventory and cost of goods sold after each purchase and sale. It gives managers more current inventory information.
An inventory system that updates inventory and cost of goods sold only at the end of the period. Cost of Goods Sold = Beginning Inventory + Purchases − Ending Inventory.
A manual count of inventory on hand. Companies use it to verify records and find missing or damaged goods.
Inventory loss from theft, damage, mistakes, or waste. It reduces inventory and usually increases cost of goods sold or a loss account.
An inventory costing method that assumes the oldest goods are sold first. Ending inventory is based on the most recent costs.
An inventory costing method that assumes the newest goods are sold first. Ending inventory is based on older costs.
An inventory costing method that uses the average cost of all similar goods available for sale. Weighted Average Cost per Unit = Cost of Goods Available for Sale ÷ Units Available for Sale.
An inventory costing method that tracks the actual cost of each specific item sold. It is common for unique or expensive items like cars, jewelry, or custom equipment.
The difference between inventory reported under FIFO and inventory reported under LIFO. It helps compare companies that use different inventory methods.
A ratio showing how many times inventory is sold and replaced during a period. Inventory Turnover = Cost of Goods Sold ÷ Average Inventory.
A measure of how long inventory sits before being sold. Days in Inventory = 365 ÷ Inventory Turnover.

front: lower of cost or net realizable value (LCNRV)
back: A rule that inventory should be reported at the lower of its original cost or what the company expects to sell it for after selling costs. It prevents inventory from being overstated.
A reduction in the recorded value of inventory when its expected selling value falls below cost. It creates a loss or increases cost of goods sold.
Goods held by one company but still owned by another company. The owner keeps the goods in its inventory until they are sold.
Shipping terms meaning ownership passes to the buyer when the goods leave the seller. The buyer usually pays freight and records the inventory once shipped.
Shipping terms meaning ownership passes to the buyer when the goods arrive at the buyer’s location. The seller usually pays freight until delivery.
A reduction in the amount paid to a supplier for paying quickly. It lowers the cost of inventory purchased.
Goods sent back to the supplier because they were wrong, damaged, or unwanted. It reduces the cost of purchases or inventory.
Shipping cost paid to bring purchased inventory to the company. It is included in the cost of inventory.
Shipping cost paid to deliver goods to customers. It is usually a selling expense, not part of inventory cost.
A method that estimates ending inventory using expected gross profit. It is useful when inventory must be estimated before a full count is available.
Long-term physical assets used to run the business, such as land, buildings, machines, and equipment. These assets are not meant for immediate resale.
A cost that improves a long-term asset or extends its useful life. It is recorded as an asset first, then expensed over time if needed.
A cost that maintains an asset in normal working condition. It is expensed immediately because it does not create a major future benefit.
Recording a cost as an asset instead of an immediate expense. Managers capitalize costs when they are expected to help the business for more than one period.
The process of spreading the cost of a long-term physical asset over the years it is used. It is not a direct measure of market value loss.
A depreciation method that records the same expense each year. Annual Depreciation = (Cost − Salvage Value) ÷ Useful Life.
An accelerated depreciation method that records more expense in earlier years and less in later years. Depreciation is based on the asset’s book value each period.
An accelerated depreciation method that uses twice the straight-line rate. Depreciation = Beginning Book Value × (2 ÷ Useful Life).
A depreciation method based on how much the asset is used. Depreciation per Unit = (Cost − Salvage Value) ÷ Total Expected Units.
Depreciation recorded for only the part of the year an asset was used. It prevents a company from taking a full year’s depreciation when the asset was not used all year.
Depreciation that records more expense in the early years of an asset’s life. It is useful when an asset provides more benefit or loses usefulness faster early on.
Removing an asset from the books because it is sold, scrapped, traded, or retired. The company compares cash received with the asset’s book value to record a gain or loss.
A gain recorded when an asset is sold for more than its book value. Gain = Cash Received − Book Value.
A loss recorded when an asset is sold for less than its book value. Loss = Book Value − Cash Received.
Exchanging an old asset as part of the payment for a new asset. The old asset must be removed from the books and any gain or loss may need to be recorded.
A long-term asset with no physical form, such as a patent, trademark, copyright, or goodwill. It can still be valuable because it gives the company rights or advantages.
A legal right that gives an inventor exclusive use of an invention for a period of time. It can help protect a company’s products from competitors.
A legal right that protects original creative work such as books, music, software, or films. It gives the owner control over copying and use.
A legal right that protects a brand name, logo, symbol, or phrase. It helps customers identify the company’s products or services.
An intangible asset recorded when one company buys another for more than the fair value of its identifiable net assets. It can reflect reputation, customer relationships, or expected future benefits.
The process of spreading the cost of an intangible asset over its useful life. It works like depreciation, but for intangible assets.
A reduction in an asset’s recorded value when it is no longer worth as much as shown on the books. It creates a loss on the income statement.
The process of spreading the cost of natural resources as they are removed or used. It applies to assets like oil, minerals, timber, and coal.
Long-term assets taken from the earth, such as oil, gas, minerals, timber, or coal. Their cost is expensed over time through depletion.
Costs spent to discover or create new products, processes, or technology. Most are expensed immediately because future benefits are uncertain.
Improvements to land that have a limited useful life, such as parking lots, fences, lighting, and sidewalks. Unlike land, they are usually depreciated.
Improvements made to rented property, such as remodeling an office or store space. They are usually depreciated over the shorter of the lease term or useful life.
A debt or obligation expected to be paid within one year or the operating cycle, whichever is longer. Examples include accounts payable, wages payable, and short-term notes.
A debt or obligation not due within the next year. Examples include bonds payable, mortgages, and long-term notes payable.
Amounts the company owes suppliers for goods or services bought on credit. It is a common current liability.
A written promise by the company to pay a specific amount, often with interest. It can be current or long-term depending on when payment is due.
Expenses that have been incurred but not yet paid. Examples include wages payable, interest payable, and taxes payable.
Cash received before goods or services are provided. It is a liability because the company still owes the customer something.
The part of long-term debt that must be paid within the next year. It is reported as a current liability.
Amounts owed related to employee pay, such as wages, payroll taxes, and benefits. These liabilities arise before the company sends payments to employees or governments.
Payroll taxes used to fund Social Security and Medicare in the United States. Both employees and employers pay portions of FICA taxes.
Sales tax collected from customers that must be sent to the government. The company holds the money temporarily, so it is a liability.
An estimated amount the company expects to spend fixing or replacing products under warranty. It is recorded when the related sales are made.
A possible liability that depends on a future event, such as a lawsuit outcome. Whether it is recorded or disclosed depends on likelihood and whether the amount can be estimated.
Likely to happen. In accounting, a probable and estimable loss is usually recorded as a liability.
More than remote but not likely. These potential losses are usually disclosed in notes but not recorded as liabilities.
A very unlikely future event. Remote contingent losses are usually not recorded or disclosed.
Long-term debt raised by borrowing money from many investors. The company promises to repay the face value and usually pay interest over time.
The amount a bond will repay at maturity. It is also called par value or principal.
The interest rate printed on the bond contract. Cash Interest Paid = Face Value × Stated Interest Rate.
The interest rate investors demand for a bond with similar risk at the time it is issued. It determines whether the bond sells at face value, a premium, or a discount.
The extra amount received when a bond sells for more than face value. It happens when the stated interest rate is higher than the market rate.
The reduction when a bond sells for less than face value. It happens when the stated interest rate is lower than the market rate.
A method that calculates bond interest expense using the bond’s carrying value and market interest rate. Interest Expense = Carrying Value × Market Interest Rate.
The bond liability amount reported on the balance sheet. Carrying Value = Face Value + Unamortized Premium or Face Value − Unamortized Discount.
A ratio showing how much financing comes from creditors compared with owners. Debt to Equity Ratio = Total Liabilities ÷ Total Stockholders’ Equity.
A ratio showing how easily a company can cover interest payments from operating profits. Times Interest Earned = Income Before Interest and Taxes ÷ Interest Expense.
The maximum number of shares a corporation is legally allowed to issue. This number is set in the company’s corporate charter.
Shares a corporation has sold or distributed to stockholders. Issued shares include both outstanding shares and treasury stock.
Shares currently held by stockholders outside the company. Outstanding Shares = Issued Shares − Treasury Stock.
A company’s own stock that it issued and later bought back. Treasury stock reduces total stockholders’ equity.
The basic ownership shares of a corporation. Common stockholders usually have voting rights and receive dividends only if the board declares them.
Stock that gives owners certain preferences over common stockholders, often for dividends. Preferred stock usually has limited or no voting rights.
A small legal value assigned to a share of stock. It is usually not the same as the stock’s market price.
The amount investors paid for stock above par value. Additional Paid-In Capital = Issue Price − Par Value, multiplied by shares issued.
Cumulative profits kept in the business instead of paid out as dividends. Ending Retained Earnings = Beginning Retained Earnings + Net Income − Dividends.
A negative retained earnings balance. It means cumulative losses and dividends have exceeded cumulative profits.
Distributions of company earnings or assets to stockholders. Dividends reduce retained earnings but are not expenses.
A dividend paid to stockholders in cash. It reduces both cash and retained earnings.
A dividend paid in additional shares instead of cash. It shifts amounts within equity but does not reduce total assets.
An increase in the number of shares while reducing the value per share proportionally. It does not change total stockholders’ equity.
The date the board of directors officially approves a dividend. This is when a cash dividend liability is recorded.
The date used to determine which stockholders will receive a dividend. No journal entry is usually required on this date.
The date a declared dividend is actually paid to stockholders. Cash decreases and the dividend liability is removed.
The accounting value of common equity per common share. Book Value per Share = Common Stockholders’ Equity ÷ Outstanding Common Shares.
A profitability ratio showing how much income the company earns for each dollar of owner investment. Return on Equity = Net Income ÷ Average Stockholders’ Equity.
Preferred stock where unpaid preferred dividends must be paid later before common stockholders receive dividends. These unpaid amounts are called dividends in arrears.
Preferred stock that can be exchanged for common stock under set terms. Investors may value it because it provides preference plus possible upside.
A financial statement showing changes in equity accounts during a period. It explains changes from stock issuances, net income, dividends, treasury stock, and other adjustments.
A correction of a material error from a previous period. It is usually reported by adjusting beginning retained earnings.
A financial statement that shows how cash changed during the period. It groups cash flows into operating, investing, and financing activities.
Cash flows from the company’s main business operations, such as collecting from customers and paying employees or suppliers. These show whether the core business is generating cash.
Cash flows from buying and selling long-term assets and investments. Examples include buying equipment or selling land.
Cash flows from borrowing, repaying debt, issuing stock, buying back stock, or paying dividends. These show how the company gets or returns capital.
A way to calculate operating cash flow by starting with net income and adjusting for noncash items and working capital changes. It is commonly used in published financial statements.
A way to calculate operating cash flow by listing major cash receipts and cash payments. It directly shows cash collected from customers and cash paid to suppliers, employees, and others.
A positive net cash flow from an activity section. It means that section brought in more cash than it used.
A negative net cash flow from an activity section. It means that section used more cash than it brought in.
Cash left after supporting operations and buying needed long-term assets. Free Cash Flow = Cash Flow from Operations − Capital Expenditures.
Cash spent to buy, improve, or extend the life of long-term assets. On the statement of cash flows, these are usually investing cash outflows.
Major investing or financing transactions that do not directly involve cash. They are reported separately because they still affect the company’s financial position.
In the indirect method, depreciation expense is added back to net income because it reduced income but did not use cash. It does not mean depreciation creates cash.

Changes in current assets and current liabilities that affect operating cash flow. For example, higher accounts receivable usually lowers operating cash flow because sales have not yet been collected.
Cash generated or used by the company’s normal business activities. It is often one of the best signs of whether the core business is healthy.
How well net income reflects real, repeatable cash-generating performance. Higher quality earnings are supported by strong operating cash flow rather than one-time gains or accounting estimates.

front: cash flow to net income ratio
back: A ratio comparing operating cash flow to reported net income. Cash Flow to Net Income Ratio = Cash Flow from Operations ÷ Net Income.
Comparing financial statement amounts across time to see increases or decreases. Percentage Change = (Current Period − Base Period) ÷ Base Period × 100.
Comparing each financial statement item to a key total in the same period. On an income statement, each item is often shown as a percentage of sales.
Studying patterns in financial data over several periods. Managers use it to spot improvement, decline, or unusual changes.
A financial statement that expresses each line item as a percentage of a base amount. It makes companies of different sizes easier to compare.
Using relationships between financial statement numbers to evaluate performance, risk, efficiency, or liquidity. Ratios help turn raw accounting numbers into business signals.
Ratios that measure a company’s ability to pay short-term obligations. Common examples include the current ratio and quick ratio.
A liquidity ratio showing whether current assets are enough to cover current liabilities. Current Ratio = Current Assets ÷ Current Liabilities.
A stricter liquidity ratio that focuses on assets that can turn into cash quickly. Quick Ratio = (Cash + Short-Term Investments + Accounts Receivable) ÷ Current Liabilities.
Another name for the quick ratio. Acid-Test Ratio = Quick Assets ÷ Current Liabilities.
A ratio showing how quickly the company collects from customers. Accounts Receivable Turnover = Net Credit Sales ÷ Average Accounts Receivable.
The average number of days it takes to collect receivables. Days Sales Outstanding = 365 ÷ Accounts Receivable Turnover.
A ratio showing how many times inventory is sold and replaced during a period. Inventory Turnover = Cost of Goods Sold ÷ Average Inventory.
The average number of days inventory is held before being sold. Days in Inventory = 365 ÷ Inventory Turnover.
A ratio showing how efficiently assets generate sales. Asset Turnover = Net Sales ÷ Average Total Assets.
Ratios that measure how effectively a company turns sales, assets, or equity into profit. They help managers judge whether the business model is producing enough return.
A ratio showing how much of each sales dollar remains after covering the cost of goods sold. Gross Profit Margin = Gross Profit ÷ Net Sales × 100.
A ratio showing how much of each sales dollar remains after operating expenses but before interest and taxes. Operating Profit Margin = Operating Income ÷ Net Sales × 100.
A ratio showing how much of each sales dollar becomes net income. Net Profit Margin = Net Income ÷ Net Sales × 100.
A ratio showing how effectively assets generate profit. Return on Assets = Net Income ÷ Average Total Assets.
A ratio showing how much profit the company earns for each dollar of stockholders’ equity. Return on Equity = Net Income ÷ Average Stockholders’ Equity.
A market ratio showing how much investors are willing to pay for each dollar of earnings. Price-Earnings Ratio = Market Price per Share ÷ Earnings per Share.
Ratios that measure a company’s ability to survive long term and pay long-term obligations. They focus on debt levels and ability to cover interest.
A ratio showing the percentage of assets financed by liabilities. Debt Ratio = Total Liabilities ÷ Total Assets.
A ratio comparing creditor financing to owner financing. Debt to Equity Ratio = Total Liabilities ÷ Total Stockholders’ Equity.
A ratio showing how many times operating income can cover interest expense. Times Interest Earned = Income Before Interest and Taxes ÷ Interest Expense.
Another name for times interest earned. It measures how safely the company can make required interest payments.
The accounting value of common equity for each common share outstanding. Book Value per Share = Common Stockholders’ Equity ÷ Outstanding Common Shares.
The amount of profit earned for each share of common stock. Earnings per Share = Net Income Available to Common Stockholders ÷ Weighted-Average Common Shares Outstanding.
Comparing financial results across periods, companies, or industry averages. It helps managers judge whether performance is good, bad, improving, or declining.
Comparing a company’s results to competitors, industry standards, or best-in-class performers. Managers use it to set targets and identify performance gaps.
